question: What does 'everlasting gospel' (euangelion aionion) mean in Rev 14:6? Is this the same gospel Paul preached, or a different one? What does the anarthrous construction imply? How does euangelisai connect to the OT basar vocabulary chain (Isa 40:9, 52:7, 61:1)? What is the content of a gospel called 'everlasting'? How does the imperial euangelia thesis relate? Does it pass the Galatians 1:6-9 test?

topics.name: GOSPEL
topics.entry_text: GOSPEL — Called: Gospel of the Kingdom (Mat 4:23; 24:14), Gospel of God (Rom 1:1; 15:16; 1Th 2:8; 1Ti 1:11; 1Pe 4:17), Gospel of Jesus Christ (Mrk 1:1), Gospel of Christ (Rom 1:16; 1Co 9:12; Gal 1:7; Php 1:27), Dispensation of the Grace of God (Eph 3:2), Gospel of Salvation (Eph 1:13), Gospel of Peace (Eph 6:15), Kingdom of God (Luk 16:16), Glorious Gospel of Christ (2Co 4:4), Preaching of Jesus Christ (Rom 16:25), Mystery of Christ (Eph 3:4), Mystery of the Gospel (Eph 6:19), Word of God (1Th 2:13), Word of Christ (Col 3:16), Word of Salvation (Act 13:26), Word of Reconciliation (2Co 5:19), Word of Truth (2Co 6:7; Eph 1:13), Word of Faith (Rom 10:8), Word of Life (Php 2:16), Ministration of the Spirit (2Co 3:8), Doctrine According to Godliness (1Ti 6:3), Form of Sound Words (2Ti 1:13). Unclassified Scriptures include Isa 40:8; Mat 4:23; 24:14; Mrk 1:14-15; Luk 2:10-14; 4:18-19; Act 10:36; Rom 1:16-17; 10:15-18; 16:25-26; 1Co 15:1-2; Gal 3:8; Eph 1:13-14; 3:8-11; Col 1:5-6,23,26-29; Heb 4:2; 1Pe 1:23; Jde 1:3; Rev 14:6-7. Prophecies: Isa 40:9; 41:27; 52:7; 61:1-3.

topics.name: PREACHING
topics.entry_text: PREACHING — The act of exhorting, prophesying, reproving, teaching. The Gospel of the Kingdom of God is the subject of Christ's preaching (Mrk 1:14-15; Luk 8:1). Christ crucified and risen is the burden of Paul's (Act 17:3). Unclassified Scriptures include 1Co 1:17-18,21,23,27-29; 2:1-8; Col 1:23-29; 1Th 1:5; Tit 1:3.

topics.name: CIRCUMCISION
topics.subtopic: Institution / Necessity falsely taught / Abrogated / Figurative
topics.entry_text: CIRCUMCISION — Institution of (Gen 17:10-14; Lev 12:3). A seal of righteousness (Rom 2:25-29; 4:11). Necessity of, falsely taught by Judaizing Christians (Act 15:1). Abrogated (Act 15:5-29; Rom 3:30; 4:9-11; 1Co 7:18-19; Gal 2:3-4; 5:2-11; 6:12; Eph 2:11,15; Col 2:11; 3:11). Paul's argument against continuance (Rom 2:25,28; Gal 6:13). Characterized by Paul as a yoke (Act 15:10). Figurative (Exo 6:12; Deu 10:16; 30:6; Jer 4:4; Rom 2:28-29; Php 3:3; Col 2:11).

topics.name: WORSHIP
topics.subtopic: To be rendered only to God
topics.entry_text: WORSHIP — To be rendered only to God (Exo 20:3; Deu 5:7; 6:13; Mat 4:10; Luk 4:8; Act 10:26; 14:15; Col 2:18; Rev 19:10; 22:8). God's presence in worship (Lev 19:30; Psa 77:13; 84:4; Isa 56:7; Heb 10:25).

topics.name: CREATION
topics.entry_text: CREATION — Beginning of (Gen 1:1). History of (Gen 1; 2). See GOD, CREATOR; JESUS, CREATOR.

verses.heading: The First Angel: Everlasting Gospel Proclaimed with Judgment-Hour Content
verses.kjv_text: And I saw another angel fly in the midst of heaven, having the everlasting gospel to preach unto them that dwell on the earth, and to every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people, Saying with a loud voice, Fear God, and give glory to him; for the hour of his judgment is come: and worship him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.
verses.context: Rev 14:1-5 introduces the 144,000 with the Lamb on Mount Zion. The first angel in v.6 appears immediately after this scene of the redeemed. The three angels' messages (vv.6-12) form a unified proclamation identified by the term euangelion aionion. This is the crux text for the study: the phrase 'everlasting gospel' appears nowhere else in Scripture.

verses.notes: Euangelion aionion is anarthrous (no article). The verb euangelisai (aorist active infinitive) shares the same root as the noun euangelion — a cognate construction unique in the NT. The legon participle (v.7) introduces the direct content of the everlasting gospel: three aorist imperatives (phobethete, dote, proskynhsate) grounded by a causal hoti clause announcing the judgment hour. The creation catalog (heaven, earth, sea, fountains of waters) echoes Exod 20:11.

verses.heading: Second Angel: Babylon Is Fallen — Structural Component of the Everlasting Gospel
verses.kjv_text: And there followed another angel, saying, Babylon is fallen, is fallen, that great city, because she made all nations drink of the wine of the wrath of her fornication.
verses.context: The second angel follows the first, extending the everlasting gospel proclamation. The doubled 'is fallen, is fallen' (epesen epesen) is emphatic aorist. The perfect tense pepotiken ('has made drink') presents Babylon's corrupting influence as a completed state with ongoing results.
verses.notes: The second angel's message is structurally part of the euangelion aionion announced in v.6. The mirrored genitive chains in vv.8 and 10 (wine of wrath of fornication / wine of wrath of God) link Babylon's cup with God's judgment cup.

verses.heading: Third Angel: Warning Against Beast Worship — The Testing Truth
verses.kjv_text: And the third angel followed them, saying with a loud voice, If any man worship the beast and his image, and receive his mark in his forehead, or in his hand, The same shall drink of the wine of the wrath of God, which is poured out without mixture into the cup of his indignation; and he shall be tormented with fire and brimstone in the presence of the holy angels, and in the presence of the Lamb: And the smoke of their torment ascendeth up for ever and ever: and they have no rest day nor night, who worship the beast and his image, and whosoever receiveth the mark of his name.
verses.context: The third angel's warning completes the three-part proclamation. The conditional protasis (ei tis proskynei... kai lambanei) uses present indicatives describing characteristic/ongoing action. The apodosis uses future tenses for judgment consequences. The articular present participle hoi proskynountes identifies beast-worshipers as a community by their ongoing practice.
verses.notes: The third message is the testing truth — it distinguishes two communities by their worship allegiance (beast vs. Creator). EGW identifies this as 'the messages by which the world is to be tested' (2SM 111.3).

verses.heading: The Saints Identified: Commandments of God and Faith of Jesus
verses.kjv_text: Here is the patience of the saints: here are they that keep the commandments of God, and the faith of Jesus.
verses.context: Rev 14:12 follows the third angel's warning and precedes the harvest scene (vv.14-16). It functions as a descriptive conclusion identifying the saints by their character: hoi terountes (the ones keeping) is an articular present active participle — substantival, not imperative. The saints are described, not commanded.

greek_parsing.gloss: ἡ                    ὁ               G3588    [T-NSF] Nom Sg F
Pres Act Ptcp Nom Pl M
τὴν                  ὁ               G3588    [T-ASF] Acc Sg F

verses.notes: Tas entolas tou Theou and ten pistin Iesou are coordinated under one participle. Pistis Iesou may be subjective genitive (Jesus' own faithfulness) or objective genitive (faith in Jesus). The coordination of commandment-keeping with faith of Jesus under one participle means these are not separate identities but a single composite description. Compare Gal 5:6: 'faith working through love.' Compare 1 Cor 7:19: 'circumcision is nothing... but the keeping of the commandments of God.'

verses.heading: OT Herald: Mevasseret Zion — 'Behold Your God!'
verses.kjv_text: O Zion, that bringest good tidings, get thee up into the high mountain; O Jerusalem, that bringest good tidings, lift up thy voice with strength; lift it up, be not afraid; say unto the cities of Judah, Behold your God!
verses.context: Isaiah 40 opens the 'Book of Comfort' (chs. 40-66). The herald (mevasseret, feminine Piel participle of basar) is commissioned to announce God's arrival to Zion and Jerusalem. The content is 'Behold your God!' — a theophanic announcement. The feminine form mevasseret may personify Zion/Jerusalem as the herald.
verses.notes: The Piel participle mevasseret appears twice in this verse, establishing basar as a technical term for the divine herald. LXX renders this with euangelizo, creating the direct vocabulary bridge to NT gospel language. The command 'be not afraid' (al tire'i) uses the same root as phobeo in Rev 14:7 but in the opposite direction: here the herald is told not to fear; in Rev 14:7 the audience is told to fear God.

verses.heading: OT Herald: Mevasser on the Mountains — Fourfold Gospel Announcement
verses.kjv_text: How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings, that publisheth peace; that bringeth good tidings of good, that publisheth salvation; that saith unto Zion, Thy God reigneth!
verses.context: Isaiah 52 continues the servant-herald tradition. The mevasser (masculine Piel participle of basar) appears twice. The fourfold announcement: shalom (peace), tov (good), yeshuah (salvation), malakh (reigns). Paul quotes this in Rom 10:15. Nahum 1:15 uses nearly identical language.
verses.notes: The Piel masculine participle mevasser appears twice. The mashmi'a (Hiphil participle of shama, 'causing to hear') appears twice, alternating with mevasser. The content is fourfold: peace, good, salvation, 'Thy God reigneth!' This is the most developed OT herald passage and the one Paul directly quotes in Rom 10:15.

verses.heading: OT Herald: Spirit-Anointed Proclamation — Jesus' Mission Statement
verses.kjv_text: The Spirit of the Lord GOD is upon me; because the LORD hath anointed me to preach good tidings unto the meek; he hath sent me to bind up the brokenhearted, to proclaim liberty to the captives, and the opening of the prison to them that are bound; To proclaim the acceptable year of the LORD, and the day of vengeance of our God; to comfort all that mourn; To appoint unto them that mourn in Zion, to give unto them beauty for ashes, the oil of joy for mourning, the garment of praise for the spirit of heaviness; that they might be called trees of righteousness, the planting of the LORD, that he might be glorified.
verses.context: The herald is now anointed by God's Spirit (mashach, root of mashiyach/Messiah). The Piel infinitive levassar ('to announce good news') specifies the purpose of the anointing. Jesus quotes Isa 61:1-2a in Luke 4:18-21, stopping before 'the day of vengeance,' and declares 'This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears.'
verses.notes: Levassar is the Piel infinitive construct of basar with the preposition le. The mashach + levassar connection links the Messianic anointing directly to the gospel-herald function. Jesus' application of this passage to himself in Luke 4:18-21 creates the definitive basar-euangelizo bridge.

verses.heading: Paul's Anathema: The 'Different Gospel' Test
verses.kjv_text: I marvel that ye are so soon removed from him that called you into the grace of Christ unto another gospel: Which is not another; but there be some that trouble you, and would pervert the gospel of Christ. But though we, or an angel from heaven, preach any other gospel unto you than that which we have preached unto you, let him be accursed. As we said before, so say I now again, If any man preach any other gospel unto you than that ye have received, let him be accursed.
verses.context: Paul opens Galatians with an immediate confrontation, skipping his usual thanksgiving. The Galatian crisis is identified in Gal 2:3-5 (circumcision demanded), 5:2-3 (circumcision nullifies Christ), 6:12-13 (opponents want to glory in your flesh). The 'different gospel' is the addition of circumcision as a salvific requirement.

Aor Act Ptcp Gen Sg M

ἕτερον               ἕτερος          G2087    [A-ASN] Acc Sg N

verses.notes: Heteron euangelion (v.6) — anarthrous, using heteros (G2087, different in kind). Ho ouk estin allo (v.7) — uses allos (G243, another of same kind). The implication: what the Judaizers preach is so qualitatively different it does not deserve the name 'gospel.' Anathema esto (v.8) — present imperative of eimi with anathema — 'let him be devoted to destruction.' Paul extends the anathema to include angels (v.8), making it universally applicable.

verses.heading: Paul's Gospel: Received by Divine Revelation, Not Human Origin
verses.kjv_text: But I certify you, brethren, that the gospel which was preached of me is not after man. For I neither received it of man, neither was I taught it, but by the revelation of Jesus Christ.
verses.context: Paul defends the divine origin of his gospel against the Judaizers. This parallels the angel of Rev 14:6 — both the Pauline gospel and the everlasting gospel have divine, not human, origin.
verses.notes: To euangelion to euangelisthen hyp emou (v.11) — double article construction with aorist passive participle. The gospel has a divine source (di apokalypseos Iesou Christou, v.12).

verses.heading: Paul's Gospel Confronted: Circumcision as the Specific Heresy
verses.kjv_text: And I went up by revelation, and communicated unto them that gospel which I preach among the Gentiles, but privately to them which were of reputation, lest by any means I should run, or had run, in vain. But neither Titus, who was with me, being a Greek, was compelled to be circumcised: And that because of false brethren unawares brought in, who came in privily to spy out our liberty which we have in Christ Jesus, that they might bring us into bondage: To whom we gave place by subjection, no, not for an hour; that the truth of the gospel might continue with you.
verses.context: Paul identifies the specific issue: circumcision. Titus (a Greek) was NOT compelled to be circumcised. The pseudadelphoi (false brethren) sought to spy out their liberty in Christ and bring them into bondage — the bondage of requiring circumcision for salvation.
verses.notes: The Galatian heresy is specifically circumcision-as-salvation-requirement, not the addition of eschatological or ethical content to the gospel message.

verses.heading: Circumcision vs. Faith Working Through Love
verses.kjv_text: Stand fast therefore in the liberty wherewith Christ hath made us free, and be not entangled again with the yoke of bondage. Behold, I Paul say unto you, that if ye be circumcised, Christ shall profit you nothing. For I testify again to every man that is circumcised, that he is a debtor to do the whole law. Christ is become of no effect unto you, whosoever of you are justified by the law; ye are fallen from grace. For we through the Spirit wait for the hope of righteousness by faith. For in Jesus Christ neither circumcision availeth any thing, nor uncircumcision; but faith which worketh by love.
verses.context: Paul draws the conclusion: circumcision for justification = fallen from grace. But the positive formulation in v.6 — 'faith working through love' (pistis di agapes energoumene) — shows that Paul's gospel is not antinomian. Faith produces love, and love fulfills the moral law (Gal 5:14).
verses.notes: Gal 5:6 is the Pauline parallel to Rev 14:12. Both texts combine faith and active obedience: pistis di agapes energoumene (faith working through love) parallels hoi terountes tas entolas tou Theou kai ten pistin Iesou (those keeping God's commandments and the faith of Jesus).

verses.heading: Paul's 'First Importance' Gospel Summary — Priority, Not Exclusivity
verses.kjv_text: Moreover, brethren, I declare unto you the gospel which I preached unto you, which also ye have received, and wherein ye stand; By which also ye are saved, if ye keep in memory what I preached unto you, unless ye have believed in vain. For I delivered unto you first of all that which I also received, how that Christ died for our sins according to the scriptures; And that he was buried, and that he rose again the third day according to the scriptures:
verses.context: Paul's most concentrated gospel summary. En protois (v.3) means 'among the first things' or 'of first importance' — dative plural of protos (G4413). This is a priority statement, not an exclusivity statement. Paul gives a DIFFERENT gospel summary in Rom 1:1-4.
verses.notes: En protois: en (preposition, 'among/in') + protois (dative plural masculine of protos, G4413). The dative plural with en indicates 'among the first things' — what is most important, not what is exclusively definitive. If 1 Cor 15:3-4 were the COMPLETE definition of the gospel, Paul would be preaching a 'different gospel' in Rom 1:1-4 — which fails his own Gal 1:6-9 test.

verses.heading: Paul's Second Gospel Summary — Descended from David, Declared Son of God
verses.kjv_text: Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God, (Which he had promised afore by his prophets in the holy scriptures,) Concerning his Son Jesus Christ our Lord, which was made of the seed of David according to the flesh; And declared to be the Son of God with power, according to the spirit of holiness, by the resurrection from the dead:
verses.context: Paul's opening to Romans provides a different gospel summary from 1 Cor 15:3-4. Here the gospel includes Davidic descent, prophetic fulfillment, and declaration as Son of God through resurrection. This proves the gospel is broader than any single summary.
verses.notes: Euangelion Theou (v.1) is anarthrous — same pattern as euangelion aionion in Rev 14:6. The genitive Theou identifies source/origin. Verse 2 explicitly ties the gospel to OT prophetic promises ('which he had promised afore by his prophets').

verses.heading: Gospel as Power of God unto Salvation — Righteousness by Faith
verses.kjv_text: For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek. For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith: as it is written, The just shall live by faith.
verses.context: Paul identifies the gospel as God's saving power for all who believe. The gospel includes the revelation of God's righteousness (dikaiosyne Theou) — a concept far broader than death-burial-resurrection alone.
verses.notes: The gospel includes righteousness-by-faith theology. This connects to Rev 14:12's pistis Iesou — if the faith of Jesus is the means by which righteousness is received, then Rev 14:12 is expressing core Pauline soteriology.

verses.heading: Judgment 'According to My Gospel' — Paul Includes Judgment in Gospel Content
verses.kjv_text: In the day when God shall judge the secrets of men by Jesus Christ according to my gospel.
verses.context: Paul explicitly includes judgment in 'my gospel.' If judgment is part of Paul's gospel, then the judgment announcement in Rev 14:7 does not violate the Pauline gospel — it IS the Pauline gospel applied eschatologically.
verses.notes: Kata to euangelion mou — 'according to my gospel.' This is one of three texts (with Luke 3:18 and Rev 14:6-7) that explicitly label the combined gospel+judgment message as 'gospel.' Triple witness.

verses.heading: Gospel as Mystery Kept Secret Since the World Began
verses.kjv_text: Now to him that is of power to stablish you according to my gospel, and the preaching of Jesus Christ, according to the revelation of the mystery, which was kept secret since the world began, But now is made manifest, and by the scriptures of the prophets, according to the commandment of the everlasting God, made known to all nations for the obedience of faith:
verses.context: Paul describes the gospel as a mystery with pre-creation origin. The phrase 'since the world began' (chronois aioniois) uses the same adjective (aionios) as Rev 14:6. The gospel is made known 'to all nations for the obedience of faith' — universal scope paralleling Rev 14:6's 'every nation.'
verses.notes: Chronois aioniois (v.25) uses the dative plural of aionios — the same adjective that modifies euangelion in Rev 14:6. The 'everlasting God' (tou aioniou Theou, v.26) uses the same adjective again. The gospel is eternal because the God behind it is eternal.

verses.heading: Grace Given 'Before Eternal Times' — Aionios Reaching Backward
verses.kjv_text: Who hath saved us, and called us with an holy calling, not according to our works, but according to his own purpose and grace, which was given us in Christ Jesus before the world began,
verses.context: This is the critical text for establishing that aionios can refer backward before creation. Pro chronon aionion ('before times eternal/age-long') — if aionios here refers to pre-creation eternity, then the adjective in Rev 14:6 carries the same backward reach.

greek_parsing.gloss: Aor Act Ptcp Gen Sg M
καλέσαντος           καλέω           G2564    [V-AAP-GSM] Aor Act Ptcp Gen Sg M
Aor Pass Ptcp Acc Sg F
Χριστῷ               Χριστός         G5547    [N-DSM] Dat Sg M
χρόνων               χρόνος          G5550    [N-GPM] Gen Pl M

verses.notes: Pro chronon aionion: pro (before) + chronon (genitive plural of chronos, 'times') + aionion (genitive plural of aionios, 'eternal'). The construction explicitly places God's purpose and grace BEFORE eternal times — i.e., before creation. This demolishes the 'age-long' translation of aionios: if it means merely 'age-long' here, then grace was given before a single historical age, which is incoherent. DeSilva's proposal to translate Rev 14:6 as 'age-long accession announcement' fails this test.

verses.heading: Chosen Before the Foundation of the World — Pre-Creation Gospel Purpose
verses.kjv_text: According as he hath chosen us in him before the foundation of the world, that we should be holy and without blame before him in love:
verses.context: Paul traces election to before the foundation of the world (pro kataboles kosmou). This is one of four independent NT witnesses to the gospel's pre-creation origin (with 2 Tim 1:9, 1 Pet 1:20, Rev 13:8).
verses.notes: Pro kataboles kosmou — 'before the laying down of the world.' The gospel plan is not a reaction to sin but a pre-creation purpose.

verses.heading: Lamb Foreordained Before the Foundation of the World
verses.kjv_text: Forasmuch as ye know that ye were not redeemed with corruptible things, as silver and gold, from your vain conversation received by tradition from your fathers; But with the precious blood of Christ, as of a lamb without blemish and without spot: Who verily was foreordained before the foundation of the world, but was manifest in these last times for you,
verses.context: Peter confirms the pre-creation origin of the gospel plan. The Lamb was foreordained (proegnosmenou) before the foundation of the world (pro kataboles kosmou) but manifested in the last times.
verses.notes: Independent witness to the pre-creation gospel plan, confirming the 'everlasting' character of the gospel in Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Lamb Slain from the Foundation of the World
verses.kjv_text: And all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world.
verses.context: The Lamb is described as 'slain from the foundation of the world' (apo kataboles kosmou). This text immediately precedes the three angels' messages in Rev 14, connecting the beast-worship crisis to the pre-creation gospel purpose.
verses.notes: The phrase 'from the foundation of the world' links directly to the 'everlasting' character of the gospel in 14:6. The gospel is everlasting because the Lamb was slain (in purpose) from the world's foundation.

verses.heading: Eternal Life Promised Before the World Began — Second Pro Chronon Aionion
verses.kjv_text: In hope of eternal life, which God, that cannot lie, promised before the world began; But hath in due times manifested his word through preaching, which is committed unto me according to the commandment of God our Saviour;
verses.context: The same phrase pro chronon aionion appears here as in 2 Tim 1:9. God promised eternal life before eternal times — confirming the backward reach of aionios. The gospel was 'manifested through preaching' at the right time.
verses.notes: Second occurrence of pro chronon aionion in the Pastorals. Zoes aioniou ('eternal life') uses the same adjective that modifies euangelion in Rev 14:6. If aionios means merely 'age-long,' then the life God promises is temporary — which no interpreter accepts.

verses.heading: Paul Quoting Isa 52:7 — The Basar-Euangelizo Bridge in Action
verses.kjv_text: And how shall they preach, except they be sent? as it is written, How beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace, and bring glad tidings of good things!
verses.context: Paul explicitly quotes Isa 52:7, using euangelizo (present middle participle euangelizomenon) for the Hebrew basar. This is the definitive NT confirmation that Isaiah's mevasser and the apostolic euangelizo refer to the same proclamation tradition.

greek_parsing.gloss: γέγραπται            γράφω           G1125    [V-RPI-3S] Perf Pass Ind Sg thirdPers
ὡραῖοι               ὡραῖος          G5611    [A-NPM] Nom Pl M
Pres Mid Ptcp Gen Pl M

verses.notes: The Greek euangelizomenon agatha ('preaching good things') directly translates the Hebrew mevasser tov. Paul's quotation proves the basar-euangelizo chain is not a modern scholarly construction but an apostolic conviction.

verses.heading: Jesus Quotes Isa 61:1 — 'This Day Is This Scripture Fulfilled'
verses.kjv_text: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he hath anointed me to preach the gospel to the poor; he hath sent me to heal the brokenhearted, to preach deliverance to the captives, and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty them that are bruised, To preach the acceptable year of the Lord. And he closed the book, and he gave it again to the minister, and sat down. And the eyes of all them that were in the synagogue were fastened on him. And he began to say unto them, This day is this scripture fulfilled in your ears.
verses.context: Jesus reads Isa 61:1-2a in the Nazareth synagogue and declares its fulfillment in himself. He uses euangelisasthai for Isaiah's levassar, creating the personal basar-euangelizo bridge. He stops before 'the day of vengeance' — distinguishing his first advent mission from the judgment aspect.
verses.notes: Jesus applies the OT mevasser role to himself, creating the ultimate bridge between OT basar and NT euangelizo. The angel of Rev 14:6 uses both noun and verb from the same root — echoing Jesus' own self-identification as the gospel-herald.

verses.heading: Proeuangelizomai: The Scripture 'Gospeled Beforehand' to Abraham
verses.kjv_text: And the scripture, foreseeing that God would justify the heathen through faith, preached before the gospel unto Abraham, saying, In thee shall all nations be blessed.
verses.context: Paul uses the hapax legomenon proeuangelizomai (G4283) — a word he appears to have coined — to assert that the gospel existed in the patriarchal era. The word itself IS the argument: the gospel was 'gospeled-beforehand.'
verses.notes: Proeuangelizomai: pro (before) + euangelizo (preach gospel). Hapax legomenon — appears only here in all of Scripture. Paul coins a new word to express that the gospel existed before the NT era. The content is justification of the Gentiles through faith — which includes judgment (Abraham's faith was credited as righteousness before the law).

verses.heading: The Same Gospel Preached to OT Israel and NT Believers
verses.kjv_text: For unto us was the gospel preached, as well as unto them: but the word preached did not profit them, not being mixed with faith in them that heard it.
verses.context: The author of Hebrews explicitly states that the same gospel (euangelismenoi) was preached to both the wilderness generation and NT believers. Kathaper kakeinoi ('just as also they') makes the comparison direct and unambiguous.
verses.notes: This is the most explicit NT statement of gospel continuity across testaments. The gospel is one message from Moses to the apostles — confirming the 'everlasting' character of Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Paul at Athens: Element-by-Element Parallel to the First Angel's Message
verses.kjv_text: Then Paul stood in the midst of Mars' hill, and said, Ye men of Athens, I perceive that in all things ye are too superstitious. For as I passed by, and beheld your devotions, I found an altar with this inscription, TO THE UNKNOWN GOD. Whom therefore ye ignorantly worship, him declare I unto you. God that made the world and all things therein, seeing that he is Lord of heaven and earth, dwelleth not in temples made with hands; Neither is worshipped with men's hands, as though he needed any thing, seeing he giveth to all life, and breath, and all things; And hath made of one blood all nations of men for to dwell on all the face of the earth, and hath determined the times before appointed, and the bounds of their habitation; That they should seek the Lord, if haply they might feel after him, and find him, though he be not far from every one of us: For in him we live, and move, and have our being; as certain also of your own poets have said, For we are also his offspring. Forasmuch then as we are the offspring of God, we ought not to think that the Godhead is like unto gold, or silver, or stone, graven by art and man's device. And the times of this ignorance God winked at; but now commandeth all men every where to repent: Because he hath appointed a day, in the which he will judge the world in righteousness by that man whom he hath ordained; whereof he hath given assurance unto all men, in that he hath raised him from the dead.
verses.context: Paul's Areopagus sermon contains every element of the first angel's message: Creator God (v.24 = Rev 14:7), universal scope (vv.26,30 = Rev 14:6), judgment (v.31 = Rev 14:7), repentance (v.30 = Rev 14:7). If Paul's Athens sermon IS the gospel preached to Gentiles, then Rev 14:7's content IS the gospel.
verses.notes: Element-by-element correspondence: (1) Creator God who made world and all things (Acts 17:24) = worship him who made heaven and earth (Rev 14:7). (2) Universal scope: all nations (Acts 17:26,30) = every nation, kindred, tongue, people (Rev 14:6). (3) Judgment: appointed a day to judge the world (Acts 17:31) = the hour of his judgment is come (Rev 14:7). (4) Repentance: commandeth all men everywhere to repent (Acts 17:30) = fear God and give glory to him (Rev 14:7). If the Answering Adventism argument were correct (that the gospel is exclusively 1 Cor 15:3-4), then Paul himself violated Gal 1:6-9 at Athens.

verses.heading: Beginning of THE Gospel — Articular Euangelion and Imperial Counter-Proclamation
verses.kjv_text: The beginning of the gospel of Jesus Christ, the Son of God;
verses.context: Mark opens with arche tou euangeliou Iesou Christou Huiou Theou — articular euangelion in the genitive. The phrase 'beginning of the gospel' directly echoes and subverts the Priene inscription (9 BC), which called Augustus's birthday 'the beginning of the good tidings (euangelion) for the world.' Mark's counter-proclamation: the true euangelion belongs to Jesus Christ, Son of God, not to Caesar.
verses.notes: Articular tou euangeliou contrasts with anarthrous euangelion in Rev 14:6. Mark uses the article because he is identifying the specific, well-known gospel at the outset of his narrative. The imperial parallel is significant but serves to reinforce, not replace, the theological content.

verses.heading: Jesus Preaching the Gospel of the Kingdom — Kerysso + Legon Paralleling Rev 14:6-7
verses.kjv_text: Now after that John was put in prison, Jesus came into Galilee, preaching the gospel of the kingdom of God, And saying, The time is fulfilled, and the kingdom of God is at hand: repent ye, and believe the gospel.
verses.context: Jesus preaches 'the gospel of the kingdom of God' — articular euangelion. His message is introduced by kerysso + legon (preaching and saying), structurally parallel to Rev 14:6-7 where the angel has euangelion + legon. Jesus preached this gospel BEFORE his death, burial, and resurrection — proving the gospel is not limited to those events.

greek_parsing.gloss: τὴν                  ὁ               G3588    [T-ASF] Acc Sg F
Pres Act Ptcp Nom Sg M

verses.notes: To euangelion tou Theou (v.14) — articular. Legon (v.15) — present active participle introducing direct discourse, same construction as Rev 14:7. The content: time fulfilled + kingdom at hand + repent + believe. If the gospel is only 1 Cor 15:3-4, Jesus never preached the gospel during his earthly ministry.

verses.heading: Gospel of the Kingdom Preached to All Nations — Universal Scope Parallel
verses.kjv_text: And this gospel of the kingdom shall be preached in all the world for a witness unto all nations; and then shall the end come.
verses.context: Jesus prophesies that 'this gospel of the kingdom' will be preached 'in all the world for a witness unto all nations.' The universal scope (pases tes oikoumenes... pasin tois ethnesin) matches Rev 14:6's 'every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people.' Both use euangelion with worldwide proclamation scope.
verses.notes: Euangelion tes basileias (gospel of the kingdom) vs. euangelion aionion (everlasting gospel). Both use euangelion with universal scope. The 'kingdom' designation highlights the reign of God; the 'everlasting' designation highlights its temporal extent. Same gospel, different facets.

verses.heading: The Mystery of God Declared (Euangelise) to the Prophets
verses.kjv_text: But in the days of the voice of the seventh angel, when he shall begin to sound, the mystery of God should be finished, as he hath declared to his servants the prophets.
verses.context: Rev 10:7 uses euangelise (aorist active indicative of euangelizo) — the same verb root as euangelisai in Rev 14:6. The 'mystery of God' that should be finished is linked to what God declared (euangelise) to the prophets. Meyer connects the two passages: the euangelisai of Rev 14:6 fulfills the euangelise of Rev 10:7.
verses.notes: Euangelise (Rev 10:7) and euangelisai (Rev 14:6) share the same root. The mystery of God declared to the prophets connects to the everlasting gospel proclaimed by the angel. The prophetic link is through the OT basar tradition — God declared (euangelise) to the prophets what the angel now proclaims (euangelisai).

verses.heading: OT Structural Parallel: Fear God + Keep Commandments + Judgment = Rev 14:7,12
verses.kjv_text: Let us hear the conclusion of the whole matter: Fear God, and keep his commandments: for this is the whole duty of man. For God shall bring every work into judgment, with every secret thing, whether it be good, or whether it be evil.
verses.context: Ecclesiastes closes with a triad that exactly matches the first angel's message: (1) Fear God (= Rev 14:7 phobethete ton Theon), (2) Keep his commandments (= Rev 14:12 hoi terountes tas entolas tou Theou), (3) Judgment on every work (= Rev 14:7 elthen he hora tes kriseos autou). This structural parallel confirms that the everlasting gospel's content is not novel but coextensive with the OT duty of man.
verses.notes: The exact triad match: Fear God (yare Elohim = phobethete ton Theon), keep commandments (shamor mitsvotav = terountes tas entolas tou Theou), judgment (mishpat = krisis). This is one of the strongest OT structural parallels to Rev 14:6-12.

verses.heading: Sabbath Commandment with Creation Language Paralleling Rev 14:7
verses.kjv_text: Remember the sabbath day, to keep it holy. Six days shalt thou labour, and do all thy work: But the seventh day is the sabbath of the LORD thy God: in it thou shalt not do any work, thou, nor thy son, nor thy daughter, thy manservant, nor thy maidservant, nor thy cattle, nor thy stranger that is within thy gates: For in six days the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea, and all that in them is, and rested the seventh day: wherefore the LORD blessed the sabbath day, and hallowed it.
verses.context: Exod 20:11 provides the creation catalog that Rev 14:7 echoes: 'the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea.' Rev 14:7 has 'him that made heaven, and earth, and the sea, and the fountains of waters.' The structural overlap connects the Creator-worship imperative of Rev 14:7 to the Sabbath commandment.
verses.notes: Creation language comparison — Exod 20:11: asah YHWH et-hashamayim ve-et-ha'arets et-hayyam ('the LORD made heaven and earth, the sea'). Rev 14:7: to poiesanti ton ouranon kai ten gen kai thalassan ('the one who made heaven and earth and sea'). The Hebrew asah and Greek poieo are cognate translation equivalents. Rev 14:7 adds 'fountains of waters' where Exod 20:11 has 'all that in them is.' The parallel strongly suggests the everlasting gospel includes a call to Sabbath worship as part of Creator worship.

verses.heading: Beast Worship Context: Imperial Vocabulary Subverted
verses.kjv_text: And they worshipped the dragon which gave power unto the beast: and they worshipped the beast, saying, Who is like unto the beast? who is able to make war with him? And there was given unto him a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies; and power was given unto him to continue forty and two months. And he opened his mouth in blasphemy against God, to blaspheme his name, and his tabernacle, and them that dwell in heaven. And it was given unto him to make war with the saints, and to overcome them: and power was given him over all kindreds, and tongues, and nations. And all that dwell upon the earth shall worship him, whose names are not written in the book of life of the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world.
verses.context: Rev 13 immediately precedes the three angels' messages. The beast demands universal worship (proskyneo, v.4,8) from 'all kindreds, and tongues, and nations' (v.7) — the same fourfold formula as Rev 14:6. The first angel's message (worship the Creator) directly answers the beast's claim (worship the beast). This is the sustained counter-imperial pattern in Revelation.
verses.notes: The imperial euangelia thesis finds its strongest support here: the beast uses imperial vocabulary (proskyneo, 'all nations'), and the angel counters with the true euangelion directed to the same audience. John consistently subverts imperial claims throughout Revelation.

verses.heading: Lamb as King of Kings — Imperial Title Subversion
verses.kjv_text: These shall make war with the Lamb, and the Lamb shall overcome them: for he is Lord of lords, and King of kings: and they that are with him are called, and chosen, and faithful.
verses.context: The Lamb bears the imperial title 'King of kings, and Lord of lords' (also Rev 19:16). Roman emperors claimed these titles. John's application to the Lamb is deliberate counter-proclamation.
verses.notes: Kyrios kyrion kai basileus basileon — imperial titles applied to the Lamb. This confirms the sustained counter-imperial vocabulary pattern in Revelation that contextualizes euangelion in Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Jesus as Faithful Witness and Prince of the Kings of the Earth
verses.kjv_text: And from Jesus Christ, who is the faithful witness, and the first begotten of the dead, and the prince of the kings of the earth. Unto him that loved us, and washed us from our sins in his own blood,
verses.context: John opens Revelation by identifying Jesus with imperial-subverting titles: 'faithful witness' (ho martys ho pistos), 'first begotten of the dead' (ho prototokos ton nekron), and 'prince of the kings of the earth' (ho archon ton basileon tes ges). These titles establish the counter-imperial framework from the start.
verses.notes: Ho archon ton basileon tes ges directly subverts Roman claims to universal sovereignty. The Revelation context makes euangelion in 14:6 both a theological and a political counter-proclamation.

verses.heading: Non-Salvific Basar: Battlefield Tidings of Absalom's Death
verses.kjv_text: Then said Ahimaaz the son of Zadok, Let me now run, and bear the king tidings, how that the LORD hath avenged him of his enemies. And Joab said unto him, Thou shalt not bear tidings this day, but thou shalt bear tidings another day: but this day thou shalt bear no tidings, because the king's son is dead.
verses.context: The Expositor's Greek Testament cites this passage for the 'neutral sense' of euangelisai in Rev 14:6. However, the prophetic usage of basar (Isa 40:9, 52:7, 61:1) had long since transformed the word from neutral 'tidings' to theologically charged 'good news of God's saving action.' The Expositor's argument ignores the LXX translation history.
verses.notes: Basar appears three times in v.19-20. This is the narrative/military usage of basar — bringing news of battle outcome. The prophetic transformation of basar (especially in Isaiah) moves the word from military context to theological herald. By the NT period, euangelizo was thoroughly technical for gospel proclamation.

verses.heading: John the Baptist's Judgment Preaching Labeled Euangelizo
verses.kjv_text: And many other things in his exhortation preached he unto the people.
verses.context: Luke uses euangelizo (euengelizeto, imperfect middle) to describe John the Baptist's preaching, which in the preceding verses (3:7-17) includes warnings of judgment (ax laid at the root, unquenchable fire). Luke labels this combined judgment-and-gospel preaching as euangelizo — proving that judgment preaching IS gospel preaching.
verses.notes: This is one of three NT texts that explicitly label combined gospel+judgment preaching as 'gospel' (with Rom 2:16 and Rev 14:6-7). If John's judgment preaching counts as euangelizo, then the first angel's judgment announcement counts as euangelion.

verses.heading: Circumcision Is Nothing — But Keeping God's Commandments Is
verses.kjv_text: Circumcision is nothing, and uncircumcision is nothing, but the keeping of the commandments of God.
verses.context: Paul explicitly nullifies circumcision while AFFIRMING commandment-keeping. This is the exact position of Rev 14:12. If Paul includes commandment-keeping without violating Gal 1:6-9, then Rev 14:12's commandment-keeping does not violate it either.
verses.notes: Alla teresis entolon Theou — 'but keeping of commandments of God.' The word teresis (keeping) shares the root with terountes in Rev 14:12 (hoi terountes tas entolas tou Theou). Paul's own language bridges Galatians and Revelation.

verses.heading: Aionios Modifying Both Punishment and Life — The Matt 25:46 Test
verses.kjv_text: And these shall go away into everlasting punishment: but the righteous into life eternal.
verses.context: Aionios modifies BOTH kolasin (punishment) and zoen (life) in the same sentence. If aionios means 'age-long' (temporary) for punishment, it means 'age-long' (temporary) for life — which no interpreter accepts. This constrains the semantic range: aionios must carry the same temporal force on both sides.
verses.notes: Kolasin aionion... zoen aionion — the adjective aionios appears in both phrases with the same force. This is the strongest argument against DeSilva's 'age-long' translation of aionion in Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Blood of the Everlasting Covenant — Aionios Modifying God's Covenant
verses.kjv_text: Now the God of peace, that brought again from the dead our Lord Jesus, that great shepherd of the sheep, through the blood of the everlasting covenant,
verses.context: Aionios modifies diathekes (covenant) — 'the everlasting covenant.' This confirms that aionios can describe divine realities that span all time, not merely a single historical age.
verses.notes: Diatheke aionios parallels euangelion aionion in Rev 14:6. Both use anarthrous noun + anarthrous adjective aionios. The everlasting covenant establishes the everlasting gospel.

verses.heading: The Faith Once Delivered — The Gospel as a Fixed Deposit
verses.kjv_text: Beloved, when I gave all diligence to write unto you of the common salvation, it was needful for me to write unto you, and exhort you that ye should earnestly contend for the faith which was once delivered unto the saints.
verses.context: Jude describes the gospel as 'the faith once delivered (hapax paradotheise) unto the saints' — a fixed deposit, not an evolving message. This supports the 'everlasting' character: the gospel does not change because it was definitively given.
verses.notes: Te hapax paradotheise tois hagiois pistei — the dative pistei is the faith, modified by the aorist passive participle paradotheise ('having been delivered once for all'). The gospel is a fixed, unchanging message.

verses.heading: Jerusalem Council: Circumcision as the Specific Salvation Condition Rejected
verses.kjv_text: And certain men which came down from Judaea taught the brethren, and said, Except ye be circumcised after the manner of Moses, ye cannot be saved. But there rose up certain of the sect of the Pharisees which believed, saying, That it was needful to circumcise them, and to command them to keep the law of Moses.
verses.context: The Jerusalem Council addresses the exact issue Paul condemns in Galatians: circumcision as a salvation requirement. The council's decision (Acts 15:28-29) rejects mandatory circumcision for Gentile believers, confirming that the 'different gospel' Paul condemns is works-righteousness (specifically circumcision-for-salvation), not the addition of ethical or eschatological content.
verses.notes: Ean me peritmethete to ethei tou Mouseos, ou dynasthe sothenai (v.1) — 'Unless you are circumcised according to the custom of Moses, you cannot be saved.' This is the formula Paul condemns. The Galatians test targets salvation-by-works, not gospel-content breadth.

verses.heading: Creator Worship in Revelation's Throne Room
verses.kjv_text: Thou art worthy, O Lord, to receive glory and honour and power: for thou hast created all things, and for thy pleasure they are and were created.
verses.context: Rev 4:11 establishes Creator-worship as central to Revelation's theology. The elders worship God specifically as Creator (su ektisas ta panta). Rev 14:7's proskynhsate to poiesanti echoes this throne-room worship.
verses.notes: Creator worship in Rev 4:11 precedes and frames the Creator worship in Rev 14:7. The everlasting gospel calls for the same worship that the heavenly beings already render.

verses.heading: Angel Swears by the Creator — Creation Formula in Revelation
verses.kjv_text: And sware by him that liveth for ever and ever, who created heaven, and the things that therein are, and the earth, and the things that therein are, and the sea, and the things which are therein, that there should be time no longer:
verses.context: A second creation catalog in Revelation: heaven, earth, sea (with their contents). This parallels both Exod 20:11 and Rev 14:7, confirming that creation language is a recurring Revelation motif tied to the Creator's sovereignty.
verses.notes: Rev 10:6 creation catalog: ton ouranon kai ta en auto kai ten gen kai ta en aute kai ten thalassan kai ta en aute. Rev 14:7: ton ouranon kai ten gen kai thalassan kai pegas hydaton. Both echo Exod 20:11: heaven, earth, sea.

verses.heading: Protoevangelium — The First Gospel Promise
verses.kjv_text: And I will put enmity between thee and the woman, and between thy seed and her seed; it shall bruise thy head, and thou shalt bruise his heel.
verses.context: The first gospel promise in Scripture — the seed of the woman will bruise the serpent's head. This establishes the gospel as coextensive with human history, supporting the 'everlasting' character of the gospel in Rev 14:6.
verses.notes: The protoevangelium places the gospel at the very beginning of human history. If the gospel extends from Gen 3:15 to Rev 14:6, it is genuinely aionion — spanning the entire scope of time.

verses.heading: Angelic Euangelizo at Christ's Birth — Angel Proclaiming Good Tidings
verses.kjv_text: And the angel said unto them, Fear not: for, behold, I bring you good tidings of great joy, which shall be to all people. Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men.
verses.context: An angel uses euangelizo to announce Christ's birth — 'I bring you good tidings of great joy.' This parallels Rev 14:6 where an angel has euangelion aionion euangelisai. Both are angelic euangelizo events.
verses.notes: Euangelizomai hymin charan megalen — the angel 'gospels' the shepherds with 'great joy.' The angel of Luke 2 and the angel of Rev 14:6 both perform angelic euangelizo.

verses.heading: Unsearchable Riches of Christ and the Eternal Purpose
verses.kjv_text: Unto me, who am less than the least of all saints, is this grace given, that I should preach among the Gentiles the unsearchable riches of Christ; And to make all men see what is the fellowship of the mystery, which from the beginning of the world hath been hid in God, who created all things by Jesus Christ: To the intent that now unto the principalities and powers in heavenly places might be known by the church the manifold wisdom of God, According to the eternal purpose which he purposed in Christ Jesus our Lord:
verses.context: Paul describes the gospel as involving 'unsearchable riches' — far broader than any single summary. The mystery was hidden from the beginning of the world (apo ton aionon) and fulfilled according to an 'eternal purpose' (prothesin ton aionon). The gospel exceeds any formula.
verses.notes: Kata prothesin ton aionon (v.11) — 'according to the purpose of the ages.' The gospel operates according to God's eternal purpose, confirming the 'everlasting' character of Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Vengeance on Those Who 'Obey Not the Gospel' — Gospel Demands Obedience
verses.kjv_text: In flaming fire taking vengeance on them that know not God, and that obey not the gospel of our Lord Jesus Christ:
verses.context: Paul speaks of judgment on those who 'obey not the gospel.' The verb hypakouō (obey) applied to the gospel proves it demands obedience, not merely intellectual assent. This connects to Rev 14:12's commandment-keeping as a gospel result.
verses.notes: Tois me hypakouousin to euangelio — 'those not obeying the gospel.' If the gospel demands obedience (Paul's word), then Rev 14:12's commandment-keeping is not an addition to the gospel but a response demanded by it.

verses.heading: The Judaizers' True Motive: Glory in Your Flesh
verses.kjv_text: As many as desire to make a fair shew in the flesh, they constrain you to be circumcised; only lest they should suffer persecution for the cross of Christ. For neither they themselves who are circumcised keep the law; but desire to have you circumcised, that they may glory in your flesh.
verses.context: Paul identifies the Judaizers' motive: avoiding persecution and glorying in flesh. The heresy is not adding eschatological content to the gospel but imposing circumcision as a works-righteousness salvation condition.
verses.notes: The Judaizers' 'different gospel' is specifically circumcision-for-salvation. Paul condemns the substitution of human merit for divine grace, not the inclusion of judgment, creation, or commandment-keeping in gospel proclamation.

verses.heading: Paul Establishes the Law Through Faith
verses.kjv_text: Do we then make void the law through faith? God forbid: yea, we establish the law.
verses.context: Paul explicitly affirms that faith does not void the law but establishes it. This connects to Rev 14:12's coordination of faith of Jesus with commandments of God — the same Pauline principle in eschatological application.
verses.notes: Nomon histanomen — 'we establish the law.' Paul's affirmation that faith establishes (not abolishes) the law is the theological foundation for Rev 14:12's combined commandment-keeping and faith-of-Jesus description.

verses.heading: Additional Isaiah Basar Passage — Herald Promised to Jerusalem
verses.kjv_text: The first shall say to Zion, Behold, behold them: and I will give to Jerusalem one that bringeth good tidings.
verses.context: Isaiah 41:27 is one of five Isaiah passages using basar (H1319). God promises to give Jerusalem a mevasser (one who brings good tidings). This confirms the herald tradition that culminates in Isa 52:7 and 61:1.
verses.notes: Mevasser appears here as a promised figure — God will GIVE a herald to Jerusalem. This is part of the cumulative basar vocabulary chain (Isa 40:9, 41:27, 52:7, 60:6, 61:1) that the NT inherits through euangelizo.

verses.heading: Additional Isaiah Basar Passage — Nations Proclaiming God's Praises
verses.kjv_text: The multitude of camels shall cover thee, the dromedaries of Midian and Ephah; all they from Sheba shall come: they shall bring gold and incense; and they shall shew forth the praises of the LORD.
verses.context: Isaiah 60:6 is the fifth of five Isaiah passages using basar. Here the nations themselves join in proclaiming (yevaseru, Piel of basar) the praises of the LORD. The good news extends beyond Israel — universal scope matching Rev 14:6.
verses.notes: The nations 'shew forth the praises of the LORD' using basar. The universal scope of this proclamation — nations bringing offerings and proclaiming God's praises — parallels Rev 14:6's 'every nation, and kindred, and tongue, and people.'

verses.heading: Peter Echoing Isa 52:7: Preaching Peace by Jesus Christ
verses.kjv_text: The word which God sent unto the children of Israel, preaching peace by Jesus Christ: (he is Lord of all:)
verses.context: Peter's sermon to Cornelius uses euangelizo with eirene (peace) — directly echoing Isa 52:7's mevasser mashmi'a shalom. Peter bridges the OT basar-shalom tradition to NT proclamation of peace through Christ. The parenthetical 'he is Lord of all' parallels Isa 52:7's 'Thy God reigneth!'
verses.notes: Euangelizomenos eirenen dia Iesou Christou — 'preaching peace through Jesus Christ.' Direct echo of Isa 52:7. This NT bridge confirms the basar-euangelizo vocabulary chain is not a modern scholarly construction but an apostolic conviction.

verses.heading: Mystery of God's Will + Gospel of Salvation — Gospel Encompasses Cosmic Purpose
verses.kjv_text: Having made known unto us the mystery of his will, according to his good pleasure which he hath purposed in himself: That in the dispensation of the fulness of times he might gather together in one all things in Christ, both which are in heaven, and which are on earth; even in him: In whom ye also trusted, after that ye heard the word of truth, the gospel of your salvation: in whom also after that ye believed, ye were sealed with that holy Spirit of promise,
verses.context: Paul describes the gospel as encompassing the mystery of God's cosmic purpose — gathering all things in Christ. The gospel is called 'the word of truth' and 'the gospel of your salvation' (to euangelion tes soterias hymon). This breadth exceeds any reductionist definition.
verses.notes: Three gospel designations in one passage: 'mystery of his will,' 'word of truth,' 'gospel of your salvation.' Each highlights a different facet. The cosmic scope (gather all things in Christ, heaven and earth) parallels the universal scope of Rev 14:6.

verses.heading: Gospel of Peace — One of 22+ NT Gospel Designations
verses.kjv_text: And your feet shod with the preparation of the gospel of peace;
verses.context: Paul uses yet another designation: 'the gospel of peace' (to euangelion tes eirenes). This echoes Isa 52:7 (mashmi'a shalom) and Acts 10:36 (euangelizomenos eirenen). Each designation reveals a different dimension of the one gospel.
verses.notes: To euangelion tes eirenes — articular, with genitive of content. One of the 22+ NT designations cataloged in the G2098 word study. Shows the gospel's multidimensional character — no single designation captures its full content.

verses.heading: Gospel Preached in All the World + Mystery Hidden from Ages
verses.kjv_text: For the hope which is laid up for you in heaven, whereof ye heard before in the word of the truth of the gospel; Which is come unto you, as it is in all the world; and bringeth forth fruit, as it doth also in you, since the day ye heard of it, and knew the grace of God in truth: If ye continue in the faith grounded and settled, and be not moved away from the hope of the gospel, which ye have heard, and which was preached to every creature which is under heaven; whereof I Paul am made a minister; Even the mystery which hath been hid from ages and from generations, but now is made manifest to his saints: To whom God would make known what is the riches of the glory of this mystery among the Gentiles; which is Christ in you, the hope of glory:
verses.context: Paul describes the gospel as (1) preached 'in all the world' (v.6, universal scope matching Rev 14:6), (2) preached 'to every creature which is under heaven' (v.23), and (3) a mystery 'hid from ages and from generations' (v.26). The hiddenness-then-manifestation pattern confirms the gospel's pre-creation origin.
verses.notes: Three features parallel Rev 14:6: universal scope (all the world / every creature = every nation), pre-creation hiddenness (hid from ages = everlasting), and present manifestation (now made manifest = the angel's proclamation). 'Christ in you, the hope of glory' (v.27) defines the mystery's content.

verses.heading: Harvest Scene Following the Three Angels — Proclamation Leads to the Second Coming
verses.kjv_text: And I looked, and behold a white cloud, and upon the cloud one sat like unto the Son of man, having on his head a golden crown, and in his hand a sharp sickle. And another angel came out of the temple, crying with a loud voice to him that sat on the cloud, Thrust in thy sickle, and reap: for the time is come for thee to reap; for the harvest of the earth is ripe. And he that sat on the cloud thrust in his sickle on the earth; and the earth was reaped.
verses.context: The harvest scene immediately follows the three angels' messages (vv.6-12). The Son of man comes 'on a white cloud' to reap the earth. The everlasting gospel proclamation directly precedes and prepares for the second coming. The sequence is: gospel proclaimed → world divides into two communities (v.12 vs. v.11) → harvest/judgment.
verses.notes: The structural sequence: everlasting gospel (v.6-7) → Babylon's fall (v.8) → beast-worship warning (vv.9-11) → saints identified (v.12) → Son of man comes to harvest (vv.14-16). The three angels' messages prepare for the eschatological harvest.

verses.heading: Paul's Pre-Conversion Zeal — Context for the Galatian Heresy
verses.kjv_text: For ye have heard of my conversation in time past in the Jews' religion, how that beyond measure I persecuted the church of God, and wasted it: And profited in the Jews' religion above many my equals in mine own nation, being more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of my fathers.
verses.context: Paul describes his pre-conversion life in Judaism — zealous for 'the traditions of my fathers.' This provides context for understanding the Galatian heresy: the Judaizers wanted to impose the same traditions (circumcision, ceremonial law) on Gentile believers as a salvation requirement.
verses.notes: Perisspoteros zelotes hyparchon ton patrikon mou paradoseon — 'being more exceedingly zealous of the traditions of my fathers.' Paul's former zeal for Jewish traditions parallels the Judaizers' demand for circumcision. The 'different gospel' he condemns is a return to this works-based system.

verses.heading: Glorious Gospel of Christ — Gospel Vocabulary Diversity
verses.kjv_text: But if our gospel be hid, it is hid to them that are lost: In whom the god of this world hath blinded the minds of them which believe not, lest the light of the glorious gospel of Christ, who is the image of God, should shine unto them.
verses.context: Paul uses another gospel designation: 'the glorious gospel of Christ' (to euangelion tes doxes tou Christou). The genitive chain (gospel of the glory of Christ) connects glory and gospel — the same connection as Rev 14:7's 'give him glory.' The gospel is described as 'light' that illuminates.
verses.notes: To euangelion tes doxes tou Christou — anarthrous euangelion in some manuscripts, articular in others. The 'glory' (doxa) connection links to Rev 14:7's dote auto doxan ('give him glory'). Another of the 22+ NT gospel designations.

verses.heading: Striving Together for the Faith of the Gospel — Pistis + Euangelion
verses.kjv_text: Only let your conversation be as it becometh the gospel of Christ: that whether I come and see you, or else be absent, I may hear of your affairs, that ye stand fast in one spirit, with one mind striving together for the faith of the gospel;
verses.context: Paul combines pistis (faith) and euangelion (gospel): te pistei tou euangeliou ('the faith of the gospel'). This connects to Rev 14:12's ten pistin Iesou coordinated with the everlasting gospel. The gospel has a 'faith' that believers 'strive together for.'
verses.notes: Te pistei tou euangeliou — the faith of/belonging to the gospel. Euangelion here is anarthrous in the genitive (tou euangeliou), like Rev 14:6. The combination of faith + gospel in Phil 1:27 parallels the combination in Rev 14:6+12.

verses.heading: Gospel of God + Word of God — Gospel as Divine Word, Not Human Invention
verses.kjv_text: So being affectionately desirous of you, we were willing to have imparted unto you, not the gospel of God only, but also our own souls, because ye were dear unto us. For ye remember, brethren, our labour and travail: for labouring night and day, because we would not be chargeable unto any of you, we preached unto you the gospel of God. For this cause also thank we God without ceasing, because, when ye received the word of God which ye heard of us, ye received it not as the word of men, but as it is in truth, the word of God, which effectually worketh also in you that believe.
verses.context: Paul uses 'the gospel of God' (to euangelion tou Theou) twice (vv.8-9) and then identifies it as 'the word of God' (logon Theou) which is 'not the word of men.' The gospel has divine origin — paralleling both Gal 1:11-12 (not after man) and Rev 14:6 (an angel carrying the gospel from heaven).
verses.notes: To euangelion tou Theou (vv.8-9) — the gospel belongs to God, not to human inventors. Logon Theou (v.13) — the gospel IS the word of God. Divine origin confirms the gospel is one and unchanging — the same whether preached by Paul or proclaimed by the angel of Rev 14:6.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'good message/gospel.' 77 occurrences in 74 unique verses across 17 NT books. Overwhelmingly Pauline (78% of verses). Translation: 'gospel' 94.6% of the time. Heavy accusative bias (59%). 22+ NT designations show the gospel is a multifaceted message broader than any single summary. The word is absent from Luke, John (Luke prefers the verb euangelizo). Rev 14:6 is the last occurrence of euangelion in the Bible.
word_studies.relevance: The noun in euangelion aionion — the central term of this study. The 77 occurrences, 22+ designations, and anarthrous/articular distribution are all critical for evaluating the anarthrous construction debate.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to announce good news/evangelize.' 52 unique verses (61 tokens) across 12 books. Dominant voice is middle (88%). LXX bridge from H1319 basar — 18 of 21 OT uses map to euangelizo (PMI 10.0, score 29.44). Used for Messianic proclamation (Luke 4:18, quoting Isa 61:1), apostolic mission (Acts), and angelic announcement (Luke 2:10, Rev 14:6).
word_studies.relevance: The verb euangelisai in Rev 14:6 — sharing the same root as the noun euangelion. This cognate construction (having a gospel to gospel) is unique in the NT. The verb creates the direct bridge to the OT basar vocabulary chain.

word_studies.summary: Adjective meaning 'eternal/everlasting.' 71 occurrences in 69 verses across 19 books. Dominant usage: 'eternal life' (~43 of 69 verses). Also modifies punishment (Mat 25:46), covenant (Heb 13:20), Spirit (Heb 9:14), and gospel (Rev 14:6). Can reach backward before creation (2 Tim 1:9: pro chronon aionion). Hebrew source: H5769 olam (PMI 5.67, score 26.16). KJV: 'eternal' (46.4%) and 'everlasting' (30.4%).
word_studies.relevance: The adjective in euangelion aionion. The semantic range must settle whether the gospel is 'everlasting' (reaching both backward and forward) or merely 'age-long' (a single historical period). 2 Tim 1:9 and Tit 1:2 prove backward temporal reference. Matt 25:46 constrains the range to genuine eternity.

word_studies.summary: Masculine noun meaning 'age, era, eternity.' 95 unique verses (120 tokens) across 22 books. Root of aionios (G166). KJV: 'ever' (52.6%), 'world' (30.5%). Used in the doubled formula eis tous aionas ton aionon ('for ever and ever'). Semantic range spans 'this age' (present world-order), 'the age to come' (future age), and 'the ages' (unbounded time).
word_studies.relevance: Root noun for aionios. Understanding the semantic range of aion (age vs. eternity) is essential for interpreting the adjective aionios in Rev 14:6.

word_studies.summary: Hebrew verb meaning 'to bear tidings, announce good news.' 27 tokens in 21 unique verses across 8 books. Piel stem in 96% of occurrences. Five Isaiah usages (40:9, 41:27, 52:7, 60:6, 61:1) define the prophetic herald tradition. The Piel participle mevasser functions as a technical term for an authorized divine herald. LXX renders as euangelizo in 18 of 21 occurrences (PMI 10.0, score 29.44).
word_studies.relevance: The Hebrew root behind the NT gospel vocabulary. The basar-euangelizo bridge (PMI 10.0, score 29.44) is the strongest evidence for gospel continuity across testaments. The angel of Rev 14:6 inherits the Isaianic mevasser tradition.

word_studies.summary: Hapax legomenon (Gal 3:8). Paul's compound: pro (before) + euangelizo (announce good news). 'The scripture gospeled-beforehand unto Abraham.' Paul coined this word to express that the gospel existed in the patriarchal era. The word itself IS the argument for OT gospel presence.
word_studies.relevance: Linguistic proof that the gospel predated the NT. If Scripture itself 'gospeled-beforehand' to Abraham, then the gospel is genuinely aionion — it extends backward to patriarchal times.

word_studies.summary: Adjective meaning 'another of a different kind.' 94 unique verses (99 tokens) across 16 NT books. In Gal 1:6, heteron euangelion marks the false gospel as qualitatively different from the true one — not merely a numerical alternative but a different species entirely.
word_studies.relevance: Critical for the Gal 1:6-9 test. Heteros identifies the false gospel as qualitatively different from Paul's — a different KIND of message, not merely additional content.

word_studies.summary: Adjective meaning 'another of the same kind.' 141 unique verses (160 tokens) across 13 NT books. In Gal 1:7, ho ouk estin allo clarifies: the false gospel is NOT 'another of the same kind' — it does not deserve the name 'gospel' at all.
word_studies.relevance: Paired with heteros in Gal 1:6-7 to define what Paul condemns. The heteros/allos contrast proves Paul is not condemning gospel breadth but gospel perversion — substituting works-righteousness for grace.

word_studies.summary: Noun meaning 'accursed, devoted to destruction.' 6 occurrences across 4 books. In Gal 1:8-9, the double anathema esto constitutes Paul's most severe pronouncement: anyone preaching a different gospel is devoted to divine destruction. The formula extends to angels (Gal 1:8), making it universally applicable. Also used in Rom 9:3 (self-imprecatory), 1 Cor 12:3 (spiritual discernment test), 1 Cor 16:22 (anathema maranatha).
word_studies.relevance: The severity of Paul's anathema must be understood precisely. Paul condemns preaching a gospel that is heteron (different in kind) — specifically, substituting circumcision-for-salvation for grace. The anathema does not target broader gospel content but gospel perversion.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'tidings, reward for good news.' 7 occurrences across 2 books (2 Samuel 6x, 2 Kings 1x). All in narrative contexts — battlefield tidings (2 Sam 18:20,25,27), reward for bringing news (2 Sam 4:10, 18:22), ethical obligation to share good news (2 Ki 7:9). No salvific or prophetic usage — the prophets used the VERB basar, not the noun besorah.
word_studies.relevance: The OT noun form contrasts with the NT noun euangelion. OT prophets used the VERB basar for salvific proclamation; the NOUN besorah remained in narrative/military contexts. The NT coined euangelion as the theological noun — a semantic development from Hebrew verb to Greek noun.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'judgment, judicial verdict, justice.' 47 occurrences across 14 books. Hebrew background: riyb (covenant lawsuit). In Rev 14:7, he hora tes kriseos autou — 'the hour of his judgment' — uses krisis for the divine judgment that grounds the first angel's imperatives.
word_studies.relevance: The judgment vocabulary in Rev 14:7. Krisis as the content of the everlasting gospel — the judgment announcement IS good news because it vindicates God's faithful.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to fear, reverence.' In Rev 14:7, phobethete (aorist passive imperative, 2nd plural) — 'Fear God!' — is the first imperative of the everlasting gospel. The semantic range spans terror/dread and reverence/awe. In the context of Rev 14:7, the fear is reverential worship of the Creator, contrasting with fear of the beast (Rev 13).
word_studies.relevance: First imperative of the everlasting gospel. The call to 'fear God' echoes Eccl 12:13 ('Fear God, and keep his commandments') — the same triad of fear + commandments + judgment.

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to worship, do reverence to.' In Rev 14:7, proskynhsate (aorist active imperative, 2nd plural) — 'Worship the Creator!' — is the third imperative of the everlasting gospel. Proskyneo appears 24 times in Revelation, establishing worship as the central issue of the book. The contrast: proskyneo the beast (Rev 13:4,8) vs. proskyneo the Creator (Rev 14:7).
word_studies.relevance: Third imperative of the everlasting gospel. The worship issue is the central conflict of Revelation — beast worship vs. Creator worship. The everlasting gospel defines the right object of worship.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'glory, honor, splendor.' In Rev 14:7, dote auto doxan — 'give Him glory' — is the second imperative of the everlasting gospel. Glory-giving as a gospel response connects to the LXX bridge kabad→doxazo (Hebrew 'honor/weight' → Greek 'glorify').
word_studies.relevance: Second imperative of the everlasting gospel. The call to give glory to God contrasts with the beast's self-glorification (Rev 13:5-6 — 'a mouth speaking great things and blasphemies').

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'hour, time, moment.' In Rev 14:7, he hora tes kriseos — 'the hour of his judgment' — uses hora for temporal specificity. Hora (hour) vs. hemera (day) encodes the anticipation-vs-announcement distinction: NT preachers spoke of the 'day' of judgment (Rom 2:16); the first angel speaks of the 'hour' — more specific, more imminent.
word_studies.relevance: The temporal marker of the first angel's message. The shift from hemera to hora signals the arrival of the judgment, not merely its anticipation.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'faith, faithfulness, trust.' In Rev 14:12, ten pistin Iesou — 'the faith of Jesus' — may be subjective genitive (Jesus' own faithfulness), objective genitive (faith in Jesus), or a compact genitive encompassing both. The Pauline pistis Christou tradition (Rom 3:22, Gal 2:16) parallels this construction.
word_studies.relevance: Coordinates with commandment-keeping in Rev 14:12. If pistis Iesou is subjective genitive, it expresses justification-by-faith theology — 'the faithfulness that Jesus demonstrated.' EGW identifies this as 'justification by faith is the third angel's message in verity.'

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to keep, guard, observe.' In Rev 14:12, hoi terountes tas entolas tou Theou — 'the ones keeping the commandments of God' — uses the present active participle to describe the saints' ongoing identity. Tereo emphasizes careful, watchful keeping — not mere acknowledgment but active observance.
word_studies.relevance: Identifies the saints by their practice. The present participle terountes is descriptive (identifying the saints), not imperative (commanding them). Compare Paul's teresis entolon Theou in 1 Cor 7:19.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'commandment, injunction.' In Rev 14:12, tas entolas tou Theou — 'the commandments of God' — refers to God's authoritative commands. The genitive tou Theou identifies these as God's commandments specifically (not human traditions).
word_studies.relevance: Coordinated with pistis Iesou in Rev 14:12. The commandments-of-God + faith-of-Jesus combination is the distinctive description of the saints who respond to the everlasting gospel.

word_studies.summary: Contracted superlative adjective meaning 'first, foremost, chief.' 105 occurrences. In 1 Cor 15:3, en protois means 'among the first things' or 'of first importance' — marking priority/primacy, not exclusivity. Paul signals that what follows is foundational, not definitional of the gospel's total content.
word_studies.relevance: Critical for the '1 Cor 15:3-4 as exclusive gospel definition' argument. If en protois means 'of first importance' (primacy), then 1 Cor 15:3-4 is a priority statement, not an exclusivity statement. Paul is saying 'these are the MOST important things,' not 'these are the ONLY things.'

word_studies.summary: Verb meaning 'to circumcise.' 22 occurrences across 6 books. Heaviest concentration in Galatians (7x) and Acts (7x). In Galatians, peritemno appears exclusively in soteriological polemic — circumcision as a salvation requirement nullifies grace (Gal 5:2-4). In 1 Cor 7:18-19, Paul declares circumcision theologically neutral. In Col 2:11, spiritual circumcision replaces physical.
word_studies.relevance: Identifies the specific act Paul condemns in Galatians as the 'different gospel.' The Galatian heresy is circumcision-for-salvation, not the addition of judgment, commandment-keeping, or creation theology to gospel content.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'proclamation, preaching, the thing preached.' Related to kerysso (to herald/proclaim). In the herald tradition, kerygma emphasizes the authoritative public announcement — the herald declares the king's message. Related to the euangelizo/euangelion word family by function (both describe proclamation) but distinguished by emphasis (kerygma = mode of public announcement; euangelion = the content of good news).
word_studies.relevance: Related proclamation vocabulary. The herald (keryx) model connects the OT mevasser tradition to NT apostolic proclamation and to the angel of Rev 14:6.

word_studies.summary: Hebrew noun meaning 'everlasting, forever, perpetuity.' The Hebrew conceptual equivalent of aionios. Used 199 times in OT for 'everlasting/forever' contexts. LXX consistently renders olam as aionios (PMI 5.67, score 26.16). The semantic tradition established by olam→aionios in the LXX is the background Rev 14:6 inherits.
word_studies.relevance: Hebrew background for aionios in Rev 14:6. The olam→aionios LXX bridge establishes that 'everlasting gospel' inherits the Hebrew concept of indefinite duration stretching both backward and forward.

word_studies.summary: Feminine noun meaning 'patience, endurance, steadfast perseverance.' In Rev 14:12, he hupomone ton hagion — 'the patience of the saints' — introduces the characterization of those who respond to the everlasting gospel. Hupomone implies active, courageous endurance under trial, not passive waiting.
word_studies.relevance: Introduces the Rev 14:12 characterization. The saints' response to the everlasting gospel is not mere agreement but sustained endurance — keeping commandments and maintaining faith under the pressure of the beast-worship crisis.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'mystery, hidden purpose.' In Rev 10:7, the 'mystery of God' (to mysterion tou Theou) connects to the everlasting gospel via the shared verb euangelizo. Paul uses mysterion for the gospel's hidden-then-revealed character (Rom 16:25-26; Eph 3:4-9; Col 1:26-27). The mystery kept secret since the world began is now made manifest.
word_studies.relevance: Links Rev 10:7 (mystery of God should be finished, as he euangelise to the prophets) with Rev 14:6 (having euangelion aionion euangelisai). The everlasting gospel is the finishing of the mystery declared to the prophets.

word_studies.summary: Neuter noun meaning 'mid-heaven, zenith.' In Rev 14:6, the angel flies en mesouranhemati — in the middle of heaven. This position ensures maximum visibility and audibility for the gospel proclamation. Appears only 3 times in the NT, all in Revelation (8:13, 14:6, 19:17).
word_studies.relevance: The angel's position in mid-heaven ensures the universal reach of the everlasting gospel proclamation — visible and audible to all who dwell on earth.
